headline: Scientists discover a hidden songbird after 120 years
meta_description: Researchers confirm that what was thought to be one bird species across Indonesian islands is actually two distinct species with different songs.
summary_short: A songbird on Indonesia's Babar Islands has been identified as a previously unknown species, distinguished by its unique cheerful song.
body:
For over a century, scientists believed the same songbird species inhabited two separate Indonesian island groups: the Babar Islands and the Tanimbar Islands, roughly 135 kilometers apart in the eastern Banda Sea. But a new study has revealed the truth: the birds on Babar are actually a distinct species altogether, now named the cheerful fantail. The discovery comes after researchers analyzed museum specimens, recorded bird calls, and played those recordings back to wild populations 132 times across several years. What makes this finding remarkable is how subtle the physical differences are—the cheerful fantail has only slightly darker upperparts and less cinnamon plumage than its relative—yet the two species sound completely different. While Babar's birds produce single whistles that rise in pitch, Tanimbar's birds combine short and long whistles into a much more rhythmic pattern. When researchers played recordings from one island to birds on the other, the animals simply ignored them.

This vocal divide matters profoundly. Since birds use songs to find mates and identify their own kind, the failure of these populations to recognize each other's calls suggests they would rarely, if ever, breed together—confirming they truly are separate species. The research team, led by James A. Eaton of Birdtour Asia Limited and Alex J. Berryman of BirdLife International, notes that while the cinnamon-tailed fantail was previously considered of least concern, the division into two species with reduced ranges means both populations now meet initial thresholds for threatened status.

The discovery underscores how much remains unknown even on well-known islands. Indonesia's Babar Islands saw virtually no ornithologist visits for a century until 2009—a reminder that nature's secrets are still waiting to be uncovered by those patient enough to listen.
quick_facts: Two distinct songbird species confirmed on separate Indonesian islands
Cheerful fantail identified after 120 years of misclassification
Bird calls differ dramatically: rising single whistles vs. rhythmic combinations
Researchers tested 19 museum specimens and recorded calls 132 times
Babar Islands unvisited by ornithologists for over a century until 2009
